topic.what: Comprehensive review of small intestinal bacterial overgrowth (SIBO), covering pathophysiology, risk factors, diagnostic methods, and treatment, with specific emphasis on the role of gastric acid as the primary prophylactic barrier against SIBO and the predisposing effect of hypochlorhydria, PPI use, and atrophic gastritis.
topic.how: Narrative synthesis of the clinical and mechanistic SIBO literature; documents how the acid barrier normally prevents bacterial colonization of the proximal small intestine, how its disruption (through PPIs, atrophic gastritis, or achlorhydria) leads to bacterial overgrowth, and how hydrogen breath testing serves as the primary non-invasive diagnostic tool. Reviews antibiotic treatment evidence (rifaximin, metronidazole).
topic.why_matters: Directly links gastric acid pathology (including gastritis-associated hypochlorhydria) to a defined downstream consequence — SIBO — with measurable clinical effects including nutrient malabsorption, bloating, and diarrhea. Provides mechanistic grounding for why gastric acid adequacy matters for the entire upper GI microbiome and nutrient absorption.

characteristics.what_it_measures: Degree to which the acid-as-SIBO-barrier mechanism is explained
characteristics.value: Strong — acid pH threshold, bacterial killing kinetics, and bypass conditions all described
characteristics.vs_baseline: Earlier SIBO literature focused on motility disorders as primary risk factor
characteristics.evidence: Mechanistic studies on acid killing of enteric bacteria plus clinical cohort data on PPI users
characteristics.context: Acid barrier is one of multiple protective mechanisms; motility and ileocecal valve integrity also critical

characteristics.dimension: Diagnostic sensitivity of hydrogen breath test
characteristics.what_it_measures: Accuracy of lactulose/glucose hydrogen breath testing for SIBO diagnosis
characteristics.vs_baseline: Small bowel aspirate culture (>10^5 CFU/mL) is gold standard but invasive
characteristics.evidence: Published sensitivity/specificity studies for breath testing protocols
characteristics.context: Variability in breath test protocols and substrate choice complicates cross-study comparison

characteristics.what_it_measures: Evidence quality for PPI use as a SIBO risk factor
characteristics.value: Moderate — multiple observational studies; odds ratios 2-8x elevated SIBO prevalence in PPI users
characteristics.vs_baseline: SIBO risk in general population approximately 6-15%
characteristics.evidence: Cross-sectional and cohort studies reviewed; no RCTs on PPI-induced SIBO
characteristics.context: Confounding by underlying GI disease indication for PPI use is a major limitation

good_for: Establishing the mechanistic link between gastric acid suppression and SIBO development
Clinical recognition of SIBO as a complication of hypochlorhydria and atrophic gastritis
Selecting and interpreting hydrogen breath tests for SIBO diagnosis
Justifying microbiome-aware management in patients on long-term PPIs
not_for: Definitive SIBO diagnosis without breath testing or small bowel aspirate
Populations where SIBO is driven primarily by motility disorders or anatomical abnormalities
Current antibiotic treatment protocols — rifaximin evidence has expanded since 2007
requires: Updated literature review for post-2007 SIBO treatment advances (rifaximin approval, elemental diet)
Clinical judgment on PPI deprescribing risk-benefit in SIBO-suspected patients

novelty: Provided a structured comprehensive review placing gastric acid deficiency centrally in SIBO pathogenesis
Synthesized the evidence base for PPI-associated SIBO at a time when this connection was emerging clinically
Established hydrogen breath testing as the practical diagnostic standard with documented performance characteristics
open_problems: Optimal antibiotic regimen and duration for SIBO treatment in hypochlorhydria-associated cases remains debated
Whether correcting hypochlorhydria (stopping PPI, using betaine HCl) resolves or prevents SIBO is unstudied
Methane-producing (archaeal) overgrowth requires different diagnosis and treatment — underaddressed
SIBO recurrence rates and maintenance strategies are poorly defined in acid-suppressed patients
The interaction between H. pylori-induced hypochlorhydria and SIBO risk has not been systematically studied
